draft.introduction: The Instruction of Ani is an Egyptian wisdom text from the New Kingdom (c. 1300 BCE), a father’s advice to his son on piety, marriage, temperance, and proper conduct. It is notable for including the son’s skeptical response to his father’s counsel.

translation.text: The scribe Ani says to his son Khonshotep: Take a wife while you are young, that she may bear you a son. Raise him well, and be thankful to God. Do not sit in a drinking house, lest evil words come from your mouth. Guard your speech; a man’s ruin is on his tongue. Do not raise your voice in the house of God; he loves silence.
translation.original_text: [Hieratic Egyptian text, Papyrus Boulaq 4, Cairo Museum]

translator_notes.note: Uniquely among Egyptian wisdom texts, the Instruction of Ani preserves the son’s rebuttal: Khonshotep argues that not everyone can follow such lofty counsel. The father’s response—that even animals can be trained—raises questions about the relationship between nature and nurture in moral development.
